questions.question: In a critical trauma patient, IV access should generally be obtained:
questions.rationale: Access should not delay transport; start IVs en route whenever possible.

questions.question: The external jugular vein runs from the angle of the jaw to:
options: The junction of the medial and middle third of the clavicle
questions.rationale: The EJ courses to the junction of the medial and middle third of the clavicle, draining into the subclavian vein.

questions.question: To make the external jugular more prominent before cannulation, you should:
options: Elevate the head of the bed
Apply a tourniquet to the arm
questions.rationale: Pressure just above the clavicle occludes outflow and distends the vein.

questions.question: The indication for EJ cannulation is:
options: A patient needing IV access with no suitable peripheral vein
questions.rationale: EJ cannulation is used when no suitable peripheral vein is found in an adult or child needing access.

questions.question: Intraosseous infusion delivers fluids and drugs into the:
questions.rationale: IO access uses the marrow cavity, which behaves as a non-collapsible vein with rapid central circulation.

questions.question: A common drill IO site is the:
questions.rationale: The proximal tibia (medial flat surface below the tuberosity) is a standard drill IO site, along with the proximal humerus.

questions.question: A sternal IO device places the catheter in the:
questions.rationale: Sternal IO devices are placed in the manubrium of the sternum.

questions.question: Which finding confirms correct IO placement?
options: Free flow without soft-tissue swelling and ability to aspirate marrow
questions.rationale: A stable needle, marrow aspiration, and free flow without extravasation confirm correct placement.

questions.question: In a conscious patient, before flushing an IO you should consider giving:
questions.rationale: IO infusion is painful; slow IO lidocaine per protocol blunts the pain before the flush and fluids.

questions.question: Why should the IO be flushed briskly after insertion?
options: To open the marrow channel and establish free flow
questions.rationale: A rapid flush opens the intramedullary channel so fluids and drugs can run freely.

questions.question: Which is a contraindication to IO placement in a given bone?
options: A fracture of that bone or a recent IO attempt in it
questions.rationale: A fractured bone or one with a recent prior IO attempt will leak (extravasate) and should be avoided.

questions.question: A length-based resuscitation tape estimates a child's:
options: Weight, with corresponding doses and equipment sizes
questions.rationale: The tape estimates weight from length and provides matched drug doses, fluid volumes, and equipment sizes.

questions.question: The child is measured with a length-based tape from:
questions.rationale: Measure head to heel and read the corresponding color zone for weight and reference values.

questions.question: Why is the marrow cavity reliable for access in shock?
options: It is a non-collapsible space that stays patent even in profound shock
It is only present in children
questions.rationale: The bony marrow network stays open when peripheral veins collapse, making IO dependable in shock.

questions.question: A complication of external jugular cannulation is:
questions.rationale: EJ cannulation risks air embolism, infiltration, and hematoma; secure the line and monitor the site.

questions.question: Which patient is the best candidate for immediate IO access?
options: A stable patient with good arm veins
A critically injured shock patient with collapsed peripheral veins
A patient with a minor laceration
questions.rationale: IO is ideal when rapid access is needed and peripheral veins are collapsed or inaccessible.

questions.question: Spending several minutes on scene to obtain access in a load-and-go patient is:
options: A common mistake that delays definitive care
questions.rationale: Delaying transport for access is a common error; obtain access en route for critical patients.

questions.question: If fluid infused through an IO causes soft-tissue swelling, you should suspect:
options: Extravasation/infiltration from a misplaced or dislodged needle
questions.rationale: Soft-tissue swelling indicates the fluid is leaking outside the marrow—remove and choose another site.
